critic_raw:
The methodology is carefully constructed and shows genuine awareness of AML-specific pitfalls. However, I identified two root-cause issues that materially threaten the validity of the comparison, plus one secondary concern about a prerequisite assumption.

---

**Root Cause 1: Temporal leakage via stratified (non-temporal) splitting**

The claim: Stratified random sampling at the window level keeps class prevalence and risk-tier composition comparable across splits, ensuring a fair comparison.

Why this is wrong: The data is 36 months of temporal transaction sequences. Account behavior patterns, merchant network structure, and AML typologies are not i.i.d. — they evolve. Rolling 90-day windows drawn from overlapping calendar periods can appear in both train and test sets when split by stratified random sampling rather than by time. Specifically:

1. A customer's window [Jan–Mar] could be in the test set while their window [Feb–Apr] is in the training set. The LSTM sees the overlapping Feb–Mar transactions during training and effectively memorizes account-level behavioral signatures. The aggregated baseline, drawing on the same account-level KYC attributes and aggregates, is somewhat less sensitive to this because there is no sequential memory exploitation — but the degree of leakage is asymmetric. The sequence model benefits disproportionately from memorizing account-level patterns rather than learning generalizable AML signals.

2. Merchant graph embeddings are computed from 'the training window' and held fixed, but if training windows are not temporally segregated from validation/test windows (which they are not under stratified random split), the graph embedding computation implicitly uses counterparty co-occurrence data from the same calendar period as test windows. For the sequence model, which uses graph features per transaction, this means the embedding is not a genuinely out-of-sample representation.

The correct split strategy for rolling-window sequential data is temporal: train on windows ending before month T, validate on T to T+k, test on T+k onward. Stratification is a tool for cross-sectional data; applying it to temporal rolling windows confounds temporal with cross-sectional generalization.

What would constitute evidence: (a) Report per-account leakage rate — what fraction of test-set accounts also appear in training? If high, re-run with a temporal split. (b) Compare model performance on accounts that appear only in test vs. accounts that also appear in training — if the gap is large for the sequence model and small for the baseline, leakage is operative. (c) The temporal-shift diagnostic (performance stratified by month within test set) is listed as a secondary diagnostic but would only detect shift within the already-leaked test set, not the leakage itself.

---

**Root Cause 2: The sequence model and the baseline do not receive informationally equivalent feature sets — the asymmetry may not isolate sequence structure**

The claim: The confound control isolates sequence structure as the source of gain by giving the baseline aggregated merchant graph statistics.

Why this is wrong: The proposed model receives: (a) temporal transaction sequences (ordering, timing, amounts), (b) per-transaction merchant graph features (degree centrality, clustering coefficient, community membership per counterparty), and (c) temporal statistics. The baseline receives: (a) 90-day aggregates, (b) average merchant degree and fraction of high-degree counterparties. This is not equivalent information content — it is not just a difference in sequence structure. The per-transaction graph features in the sequence model carry rich, sparse, counterparty-specific signals (community membership per merchant per transaction) that are not recoverable from the aggregate 'average merchant degree' the baseline receives. If the sequence model wins, the attributable cause could be: (i) sequence structure, (ii) richer graph feature granularity, or (iii) their interaction. The experiment cannot distinguish these.

A cleaner isolation would be: give the baseline the same per-transaction graph features flattened/aggregated with more fidelity (e.g., distribution of community memberships, percentile statistics of clustering coefficients per transaction rather than a single mean). Alternatively, run a third model: GBT on per-transaction-level features aggregated at the same fidelity as the sequence model uses them. The gap between that third model and the LSTM isolates sequence structure specifically.

What would constitute evidence: An ablation experiment — (a) baseline + richer graph aggregates (matching information content of per-transaction graph in the LSTM), (b) LSTM without graph features, (c) full LSTM with graph features. If (a) closes most of the gap vs. full LSTM, the graph feature granularity explains the gain, not sequence modeling. If (b) and (c) are close and (a) lags both, sequence structure dominates. Neither ablation is currently in the design.

---

**Secondary Concern: Prerequisite assumption about sequence length distribution**

The claim: The LSTM/transformer meaningfully exploits sequence structure across the 90-day window.

Why this might not hold: The mechanism by which sequence models outperform aggregated baselines requires that (a) sequence length is long enough to carry non-trivial ordering information and (b) the ordering is not destroyed by extreme sparsity. For retail AML, many accounts may have very low transaction frequency — perhaps 5–15 transactions per 90-day window. At this length, a bidirectional LSTM with 64–128 hidden units and transaction-level embeddings may not have enough sequential signal to do better than a well-tuned GBT on aggregated features; the model capacity is underutilized, and the inductive bias of sequentiality is not activated. High-frequency accounts (where sequence modeling genuinely helps) may be a minority, diluting aggregate metrics.

This is a prerequisite assumption, not a design flaw — but it should be checked, because if the LSTM wins overall while losing on sparse-transaction accounts, the result is narrower than advertised.

What would constitute evidence: Stratify final test-set metrics by transaction count per window (e.g., <10, 10–30, >30). If the sequence model advantage concentrates in the high-frequency stratum, the claim should be scoped accordingly.

---

**What is well-designed and should not be changed:**
- The 90-day label-lag requirement is correct and operationally sound.
- Preprocessing fit-on-train-only is correct.
- The metric choice (precision at fixed recall) is operationally justified and appropriate for imbalanced AML data.
- McNemar's test on the held-out set is appropriate.
- The scope limitation to a single institution's transaction distribution is honest.

The dominant issue is the non-temporal split applied to temporally structured rolling-window data, which creates asymmetric leakage that favors the sequence model. This is a root-cause problem, not a secondary concern.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random splitting of rolling 90-day windows creates temporal leakage because overlapping windows from the same account can appear in both train and test sets, asymmetrically benefiting the sequence model which can memorize account-level behavioral signatures.

Merchant graph embeddings computed from 'the training window' under a non-temporal split implicitly incorporate counterparty co-occurrence data from the same calendar period as test windows, contaminating the out-of-sample graph representation.

The proposed sequence model receives per-transaction merchant graph features (community membership, clustering coefficient per counterparty) that are strictly richer than the average-degree aggregates given to the baseline, so any observed gain cannot be attributed to sequence structure alone.
No ablation isolates the contribution of graph feature granularity vs. sequence modeling: a third condition (GBT with equally granular graph aggregates) is required to decompose the effect.

The mechanism of sequence model advantage presupposes sufficient transaction frequency per window; if most accounts have sparse sequences (5–15 transactions per 90 days), the inductive bias of sequence modeling may not activate, and the aggregate result will be diluted and misleading without stratification by transaction count.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random splitting of rolling 90-day windows creates temporal leakage because overlapping windows from the same account can appear in both train and test sets, asymmetrically benefiting the sequence model which can memorize account-level behavioral signatures.

Merchant graph embeddings computed from 'the training window' under a non-temporal split implicitly incorporate counterparty co-occurrence data from the same calendar period as test windows, contaminating the out-of-sample graph representation.

The proposed sequence model receives per-transaction merchant graph features (community membership, clustering coefficient per counterparty) that are strictly richer than the average-degree aggregates given to the baseline, so any observed gain cannot be attributed to sequence structure alone.
No ablation isolates the contribution of graph feature granularity vs. sequence modeling: a third condition (GBT with equally granular graph aggregates) is required to decompose the effect.

The mechanism of sequence model advantage presupposes sufficient transaction frequency per window; if most accounts have sparse sequences (5–15 transactions per 90 days), the inductive bias of sequence modeling may not activate, and the aggregate result will be diluted and misleading without stratification by transaction count.
